title: Practice Set JA4 — Nulls in Aggregation
description: Recognition track: every Spark aggregate has its own null policy, and they are not the same. Predict count('*') vs count('column'), survive groupBy on a nullable column, spot the silently wrong manual average, and recognize the 'defensive filter' that quietly drops real readings. The most subtle aggregation bugs in production code live here.

datasets.description: Shared NordGrid meter readings (20 rows). Engineered nulls: 5 in daily_kw, 2 in region, 3 in customer_type, 3 in meter_id. customer_type distribution: RESIDENTIAL 8, COMMERCIAL 5, INDUSTRIAL 4, null 3.

description.context: You are reviewing a teammate's data quality check. The full NordGrid sample has 20 rows. The teammate believes that count('*'), count('daily_kw'), and count('region') should all return 20 because 'count is just count'. You know the dataset has nulls: 5 null daily_kw, 2 null region, 3 null customer_type. Predict the actual output.
description.task: What values does .show() print for the three columns?

evidence.label: snippet.py
evidence.content:
from pyspark.sql.functions import count

result = df.agg(
    count('*').alias('rows'),
    count('daily_kw').alias('daily_kw_count'),
    count('region').alias('region_count')
)
result.show()

fields.label: What values does .show() print for the three columns?
fields.rationale: count('*') counts rows — every row, regardless of nulls — so 20. count('column_name') counts non-null values in that column. With 5 null daily_kw out of 20, count('daily_kw') = 15. With 2 null region, count('region') = 18. The difference between count('*') and count('col') is exactly the null count of that column — which is why count('*') - count('col') is a one-line null check. The misconception that 'count is just count' is the most common junior aggregation mistake; the test for whether you have it is whether you can predict these three numbers without running the code.
fields.distractorRationale: All 20 — holds 'count is just count' misconception; PySpark's count() has two distinct behaviors. 15/15/15 — believes count('*') excludes any row with a null in any column; count('*') counts rows unconditionally. Null returns — thinks count() returns null when nulls are present; count() always returns an integer.

description.context: Your teammate is writing a customer-type breakdown for the operations dashboard. The NordGrid sample has 20 rows. customer_type has three distinct non-null values (RESIDENTIAL 8, COMMERCIAL 5, INDUSTRIAL 4) plus 3 rows where customer_type is null. The teammate is sure the output will have exactly 3 rows. Predict the actual row count.

fields.label: What does print() output?
fields.rationale: PySpark's groupBy treats null as a single grouping value. All rows where the grouping column is null collapse into one group, just like rows that share any other value. Result: 4 rows — RESIDENTIAL, COMMERCIAL, INDUSTRIAL, plus null with count 3. This contrasts with countDistinct (see JA5.2), which does ignore nulls. The lesson: every aggregation function has its own null policy, and groupBy's policy is 'null is a value like any other'.
fields.distractorRationale: 3 rows — imports a SQL convention that does not apply to PySpark. 6 rows — treats each null as distinct from every other null; PySpark equality is null-safe in the grouping context. 20 rows — confuses groupBy with a row-preserving transformation; groupBy collapses rows sharing the grouping key.

description.context: A teammate's pipeline computes 'average daily consumption per region' for the operations dashboard. They wrote the average manually instead of using avg(). Output looks plausible — every region has a non-zero positive number — but the operations team complains the averages 'feel low'. The full sample has 20 rows; 5 of them have null daily_kw. The job runs successfully; output has one row per region with positive avg_kw values. The values match what sum/count would give if computed by hand. Operations independently calculated using avg() and got values roughly 25% higher.
description.task: Which line contains the bug, and what is the mechanism?

evidence.label: snippet.py
evidence.content:
from pyspark.sql.functions import sum, count, col

# Compute average daily consumption per region
averages = (df
    .groupBy('region')
    .agg(
        sum('daily_kw').alias('total'),
        count('*').alias('n')
    )
    .withColumn('avg_kw', col('total') / col('n')))

averages.select('region', 'avg_kw').show()

fields.label: Which line contains the bug, and what is the mechanism?
options: Line 8: count('*') counts all rows including those with null daily_kw
Line 11: the division should use coalesce(col('n'), 1) to prevent null denominators.
Line 7: sum('daily_kw') silently includes null values as zero, depressing the total.
Line 5: groupBy('region') misses the null region rows
fields.correctAnswer: Line 8: count('*') counts all rows including those with null daily_kw
fields.rationale: The bug is the denominator. count('*') counts all rows including those with null daily_kw. sum('daily_kw') correctly ignores nulls — built-in policy of every Spark aggregate. So the manual computation divides a null-aware numerator by a null-blind denominator. With 5 nulls out of 20, the manual average is ~75% of the correct value. The built-in avg('daily_kw') divides sum by the non-null count, producing the correct mean. Fix: avg('daily_kw') (almost always the right answer), or use count('daily_kw') in the denominator if you have a specific reason to compute manually. Lesson: every Spark aggregate has a documented null policy, and avg's policy matches the statistical definition.
fields.distractorRationale: Null denominator — count('*') always returns int ≥ 0 per group; the bug is too-large, not null. Sum treats nulls as zero — reverses sum's actual semantics; PySpark sum() ignores nulls entirely, identical to SQL SUM(). Null regions — blames a separate concern; this bug is about null daily_kw, not null region.
conceptsTested: denominator alignment with numerator's null policy

description.context: A teammate added a 'defensive' filter to a regional consumption pipeline after a downstream consumer crashed on a null region value. The pipeline now runs cleanly. Three weeks later, the regulatory reporting team flags that NordGrid's monthly consumption report is consistently ~2-3% lower than their independent reconciliation. The full NordGrid dataset has roughly 240 meters with null region values. The pipeline runs successfully; output has 4 rows (NORTH, SOUTH, EAST, WEST); the dashboard renders correctly; the regulatory team reports totals are ~2-3% lower than their independent reconciliation, consistently across multiple months.
description.task: Which line contains the bug, and what is the production consequence?

evidence.label: snippet.py
evidence.content:
from pyspark.sql.functions import sum, col

# Defensive filter: drop any row with null region
# (added after the dashboard crashed on a null value)
regional_totals = (df
    .filter(col('region').isNotNull())
    .groupBy('region')
    .agg(sum('daily_kw').alias('total_kw')))

# Write to the regulatory reporting layer
regional_totals.write.mode('overwrite').parquet('gold/regional_consumption')

fields.label: Which line contains the bug, and what is the production consequence?
options: Line 10: writing to gold/ directly skips the silver layer where data quality checks should run.
Line 7: filter(col('region').isNotNull()) silently drops the ~240 meters with null region
Line 9: sum('daily_kw') is missing a coalesce to handle nulls in daily_kw
Line 8: groupBy('region') excludes null-region rows by default in some Spark versions; the
fields.correctAnswer: Line 7: filter(col('region').isNotNull()) silently drops the ~240 meters with null region
fields.rationale: The defensive filter on line 7 looks reasonable and prevents the dashboard crash that motivated it. But it also silently drops every meter whose region is null. Those readings represent real consumption — real meters, real customers, real energy delivered — but whose region tag was lost or never set due to upstream data quality issues. The regulatory report is short by exactly that consumption. The pattern is called a silent drop: defensive filtering that fixes a downstream symptom by removing data that should have been investigated. Fix has two parts: route null-region rows to a quarantine path (e.g., 'unassigned/' for the DQ team), and ensure the regulatory report explicitly accounts for them (under an 'UNKNOWN' region label or as a separate dropped-rows metric). Defensive null handling should never be silent.
fields.distractorRationale: Skip silver — identifies an architecture concern but misses the data-loss bug; even a properly-layered pipeline that drops null regions still under-reports. Sum treats null as zero — reverses sum's semantics; sum() ignores nulls entirely, and null-daily_kw is a separate population. Spark version difference — invents a non-existent behavior; groupBy includes null groups consistently.
